primary.title: RFC 9162 Certificate Transparency Version 2.0
primary.used_for: domain-separated Merkle tree hashing and append-only checkpoint design

supporting.title: RFC 8785 JSON Canonicalization Scheme
supporting.used_for: canonicalization boundary review; current receipt digest remains narrower and is not claimed RFC 8785 compliant

finding: Lifecycle-valid peer-trigger receipts still need an append-only history commitment. A domain-separated Merkle checkpoint binds the ordered receipt digests so omission, reordering, mutation, truncation, and forged prior checkpoints can be detected when the compared histories are available.
design_implication: Publish or attest periodic tree_size plus root_hash_sha256 checkpoints after ledger validation. Verify any later history as an extension of the checkpoint before reporting aggregate coordination state.

verification.method: isolated Node runtime using connector-fetched committed logic

uncertainty: The implementation recomputes and compares available prefixes; it does not yet generate compact RFC 9162 consistency proofs or signatures.
falsification_route: produce two distinct valid event histories with the same checkpoint root
show a prior-entry mutation or reorder accepted as an extension
show divergence from RFC 9162 MTH test vectors for equivalent byte leaves
demonstrate that a forked log can evade detection despite clients comparing independently witnessed checkpoints
limits: no gossip or independent witness mechanism
full prior events are required for extension verification
receipt canonicalization is project-specific and not full RFC 8785
a valid checkpoint proves byte-history integrity, not truth of event claims
